title: Proverbs 22
subtitle: A Good Name; Rich and Poor Made by the LORD; The Thirty Sayings Begin

sections.header: Verses 1–16 — A Good Name; Rich and Poor Made by the LORD; Train a Child

heb.paragraph: A good šēm (name) is more desirable than great riches (v. 1). In Hebrew culture, a name carried the full weight of a person's character and social standing. Reputation is not vanity but social currency built through consistent integrity. The proverb reorders economic priorities: character capital outranks financial capital.

Train (ḥănōk) a child in the way he should go, and when old he will not depart from it (v. 6). The verb ḥānak means to dedicate or initiate — it is used of dedicating the temple (1 Kgs 8:63). The implication is that parenting is a consecrating act: setting the child on a specific trajectory through intentional, early formation.

refs.note: A good name is better than fine perfume — the companion saying to v.1.
A good person leaves an inheritance for their children's children — the multigenerational consequence of a good name.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The good-name comparison — consistent with the sustained 'better than' curriculum throughout chs.15-21. Character and reputation outlast and outvalue any material wealth.
Rich and poor have this in common: the LORD made them both — the levelling force of creation theology. Whatever economic differences exist, a common Creator stands behind both.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: A good name over great riches — shem (name/reputation/identity) encompasses character and legacy. The comparison to silver and gold places it in the wisdom value-hierarchy above material wealth.

The generous person will be blessed — Waltke notes that tov ayin (good/generous eye) is the Hebrew idiom for the person who sees others' needs and acts on what they see. The eye that sees generously motivates the hand that gives generously. Generosity begins in perception — the way you look at other people determines how you treat them.

A good name is more desirable than great riches — the person who sacrifices reputation for wealth has made the worst possible trade. Calvin notes that shem (name/reputation) is the social expression of character: a good name is what the community testifies about who you actually are. It cannot be purchased; it must be built through consistent covenant faithfulness.

Calvin: 'Train up a child in the way they should go — the divine appointment of parents as primary educators. The training is comprehensive: the whole orientation of the child's life is to be shaped.'

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: NET: 'the LORD is the Maker of them all' — creation theology levels all economic distinctions. Rich and poor are the same kind of being made by the same God.
NET: surveys al-pi darko (according to their way/bent) — instruction fitted to the individual child's character vs. the morally correct way. Both readings are defensible.

hist.context: The first half of ch.22 contains some of the most quotable sayings in Proverbs: the good name (v.1), the rich and poor meeting — both made by the LORD (v.2), training a child (v.6), the borrower as servant to the lender (v.7). The collection consistently attends to the poor (vv.2,9,16,22-23).

sections.header: Verses 17–29 — The Thirty Sayings: Counsel, Justice, and Skilled Work

heb.paragraph: The 'Thirty Sayings of the Wise' (vv. 17–24:22) begin here, paralleling the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope. Whether direct borrowing or shared tradition, the structure of thirty discrete units is international wisdom at its finest. Šĕlōšîm signals a deliberate literary collection, not random proverbs.

Do not move the gĕbûl ʿôlām — the ancient boundary marker (v. 28). Boundary stones marked inherited land allotments dating to the original tribal distributions. Moving them was not petty theft but an assault on an entire family's economic future and covenantal inheritance. The 'ancient' (ʿôlām) intensifies the crime: it violates ancestral trust.

refs.note: Diligent hands will rule — the diligence-leads-to-authority principle of v.29 recurs throughout Proverbs.
Hear this, you who trample the needy — the prophetic parallel to v.22's warning against exploiting the poor.

alter.source: Robert Alter, The Hebrew Bible: A Translation with Commentary — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Thirty Sayings transition — the longer units allow more sustained argument than the single-verse proverb. The shift from observation to instruction is formal as well as substantive.

Do not exploit the poor because they are poor — Alter notes the social ethics are grounded in divine advocacy. The LORD will take up the case of those who have no human advocate. What appears to be exploitation of the weak is actually an offence against the most powerful advocate possible — the God who identifies with the vulnerable.

waltke.source: Bruce K. Waltke, NICOT Commentary on Proverbs (2 vols.) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: The Thirty Sayings of the Wise begin — the transition from the terse antithetical proverb to extended instructional address. This section has structural and verbal parallels with the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope.

Do not exploit the poor because they are poor — Waltke notes the theological ground: exploiting the poor is not merely unjust but theologically dangerous. The LORD takes up their case (yarivu rivam), functioning as their legal advocate. The covenant God who is the go'el of the vulnerable will prosecute those who exploit the people he has identified himself with.
Calvin: 'The LORD will plead the cause of the poor — those who oppress the poor provoke God to be their adversary. Social ethics are grounded in divine character, not merely humanitarian sentiment.'

A diligent person will stand before kings — Calvin notes this is the practical observation that skill and diligence attract the attention of those in power. This is not flattery of ambition but honest observation: competence rises to its appropriate level of responsibility. The excellent worker does not need to seek visibility; their work will eventually attract it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET note on the Thirty Sayings discusses the structural and verbal parallels with the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope, which has a thirty-chapter structure and similar content in several places. The note surveys the scholarly debate about the direction of dependence and concludes that both texts draw on the common ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition.

The NET note on riv (take up the case, contend as advocate) explains the legal background: the LORD functions as the poor person's legal representative who will prosecute those who exploit them. The note discusses the comparable covenant-advocacy language throughout the Psalms where YHWH is the defender of the afflicted.

hist.context: The second half introduces the Thirty Sayings (vv.17-29), a formal collection with its own preamble (vv.17-21). The collection's motto is the final verse: a person skilled in their work will stand before kings. Excellence earns extraordinary access.

ane.parallel: Instruction of Amenemope (Egypt, c. 12th cent. BCE)
ane.similarity: Both texts are structured as thirty sections of wisdom instruction from a sage to his pupil. The thematic sequence and specific topics show remarkable correspondence: warnings against robbing the poor, moving boundary stones, associating with hot-tempered people, and the dangers of wealth. The Egyptian text's 'thirty chapters' matches Proverbs' 'thirty sayings' structure.
ane.difference: Amenemope grounds its ethics in Ma'at, the Egyptian concept of cosmic order maintained by proper conduct. Proverbs grounds the same ethical content in the fear of Yahweh. The theological framework differs fundamentally even when specific advice parallels closely.
ane.significance: This is the most widely accepted case of direct literary dependence between a biblical text and an ANE source. Rather than undermining biblical authority, it shows how Israel's sages engaged with international wisdom traditions while reframing them within covenant theology.

ppl.name: The righteous
ppl.text: Integrity is both compass and shield.

chapter_panels.trans: VersionTranslationNIV"A good name is more desirable than great riches; to be esteemed is better than silver or gold."NoteReputation is non-fungible. The wise person builds their name through consistent character.

src.title: Ancient Near Eastern wisdom
src.quote: These proverbs reflect Israel's engagement with international wisdom traditions, especially Egyptian.
src.note: Wisdom was a shared human enterprise across cultures.

src.title: Hezekian editorial work
src.quote: Proverbs 25:1 notes that men of Hezekiah copied these proverbs — one of the most historically specific attributions in Proverbs.
src.note: The editorial activity shows Proverbs as a living, curated collection.

rec.text: Proverbs 21-30 has been foundational to Christian virtue ethics — the practical outworking of the fear of the LORD.
These chapters have been widely cited in ethics, governance, and social teaching.

rows.label: A Good Name
rows.text: A good name is more desirable than great riches; to be esteemed is better than silver or gold. The wisdom hierarchy places character and reputation above material wealth. A good name is the social expression of the character wisdom forms.

rows.label: Rich and Poor: The Same Maker
rows.text: Rich and poor have this in common: the LORD is the Maker of them all. The levelling force of creation theology: whatever economic differences exist, a common Creator stands behind both. The fundamental human equality is theological.

tx.title: 'Sayings of the Wise' — parallels with Amenemope
tx.content: This section closely parallels the Egyptian Instruction of Amenemope. MT reads 'thirty sayings' (šĕlōšîm) in 22:20, though some manuscripts read šilšôm ('previously'). The LXX does not number the sayings. The relationship between the Hebrew and Egyptian text is not reflected in any textual variant but in the compositional parallels.
tx.note: The Amenemope connection is source-critical rather than text-critical, but it illuminates the international wisdom tradition in which Proverbs participates. All textual witnesses preserve the parallels.

debate.title: Proverbs and Amenemope: Dependence or Common Tradition?

positions.name: Proverbs 22-24 adapts Amenemope
positions.argument: The parallels are too specific to be coincidental — structural and verbal similarity indicates literary relationship.

positions.name: Shared ancient Near Eastern wisdom tradition
positions.argument: Shared tradition across cultures is equally plausible and does not require direct literary dependence.

prayer_prompt: A good name is more desirable than great riches. Consider asking God about your reputation — what would those who know you say, and does it honour Him?
